figures.caption: Figure 3 Strategic Controller workflow in one escalated planning cycle. The example shows how SPIKE collects scene evidence, checks progress, reasons over the task, and emits a short plan for Reactive Controller execution.

Figure 1 Motivation for event-triggered deliberation. Single-Strategic (a) control reasons carefully but is expensive, while Single- Reactive (b) control is cheap but brittle under drift and bottlenecks. (c) SPIKE allocates strategic reasoning only at likely bottlenecks, aiming for higher success and recovery with lower per-step cost.

Table 2 Main comparison on STARDOJO. “Recovery/Stuck Ratio” is Recovery Rate divided by Stuck Rate. Entries report mean ± standard deviation over three repeated runs. Table 6 reports the raw stuck and recovery statistics behind this ratio. Bold and underlined entries denote the best and second-best results within each method group; blue shading marks results from our method.
Figure 6 Qualitative goal-reaching comparison. SPIKE escalates and returns to low-cost local execution once the plan is stable.

Figure 4 Effectiveness-efficiency trade-off on Lite-100. The two panels compare success against token use and latency; upper-left is better. SPIKE stays on the Pareto frontier, improving success without simply spending more compute.
Table 3 Task-type success breakdown on Lite-100. Each column reports the success rate for one task family. Entries report mean ± standard deviation over three repeated runs.

Figure 2 Closed-loop event-triggered deliberation in SPIKE. The Strategic Controller performs information collection, optional Self Reflection, task reasoning, and short-horizon action proposal; the Reactive Controller uses real-time signals and retrieved memory to follow, override, or trigger replanning. The Hierarchical Memory module combines Global Memory, the State-Action Memory Bank (SA-MB), and the State-Action Knowledge Graph (SA-KG) through vector search and graph traversal, then propagates updates after action execution.

Figure 5 Mechanistic analysis of SPIKE. (a) compares controller variants by SR and tokens, showing dual control improves success at lower cost. (b) reports Budgeted SR under difficulty-dependent LLM-call budgets, testing whether event triggering delays budget exhaustion. (c) removes the two memory tracks and reports success and Recovery/Stuck Ratio.

Table 5 Framework-level and component ablations of SPIKE on Lite-100. “∆ SR” is the absolute change in Lite-100 SR in percentage points relative to the full SPIKE. Entries report mean ± standard deviation over three repeated runs.
